I want to help but I really do not know how to... Providing them support will make life safer for them, and also make the spaces be perceived as safer overall.

place_A: I took a photo of a local deli that is in my neighborhood.
why_A: This deli is a community staple, and it happens to be right in my neighborhood! It is focused on locally-sourced and locally-grown ingredients, such as bread from a neighborhood bakery and tomatoes grown at a community farm in town. It has a friendly and warm atmosphere and many of the staff members have been working there since the deli opened. It's definitely a community hub and I feel like I always see someone there whom I know. Also, I can walk to the deli from my house, so I feel like that adds an extra layer of comfort because this place is right in my neighborhood.
story_A: I think maybe original residents of this neighborhood may not feel "safe" in this deli because, in many ways, it represents gentrification of a previously poor neighborhood. This part of town used to be called "East Nasty" because it was extremely run-down, crime-ridden, and housed some of the poorest residents in my city... but in the past ten years, it's become kind of a "hipster" mecca with lots of expensive restaurants and boutiques. I think the safety of this place could be improved with products that are more accessible price-wise, as all of the sandwiches here are $10+. They could possibly have some cheaper options to appeal to people who aren't willing or able to spend that much on an artisanal sandwich.
place_B: This is a street on my neighbor and my neighborhood that connects the neighborhood to a large Evergreen Park in The Maplewood system.
why_B: The park and Maplewood system are very close to where I live. It is an extensive network and a very large park. People from all over the city use these spaces. Because parking is scarce I make a habit of walking from my house when I want to use the park or the greenway. It's only a 5-minute walk. Unfortunately, people drive too fast on the street that I walk on. Cars often slow down and honk at women or gawk. Sometimes there is visible drug dealing. There are also homeless people who are sometimes passed out on the sidewalk or experiencing psychosis. Sometimes there is a lot of trash. It feels both unsafe because of the traffic and also unsafe because of the unsheltered homeless people who are unwell and occupy the space.
story_B: This space could be more safe with traffic calming measures. The speed limit is only 25 but cars regularly go very very fast and even race on the street. There's also a need for more cleanup efforts to get rid of needles and garbage on the side of the road. There are no easy answers to unsheltered homelessness but addressing this in the city as a whole is certainly part of the solution.

It attracts bad elements and then those start to spread further into the community.
story_B: The Breezy Grove should tear this down and pave the parking lot. They should clean up the area and have more patrols to make sure that there are no vagrants hanging around. They need to repurpose and revitalize this area.

place_A: This is an exterior picture of the Riverton Public Library at Grand Valley Plaza in Riverton, New Arland.
why_A: The library has always been a place where I feel safe. It is a free location that is open to anyone and everyone in my community. The staff is empathetic and helpful. It is a place where knowledge is protected and shared with anyone who wants it. The building itself is a shelter from the elements. A great place to go on a rainy day or during the harsh winter months. In the summer the conditioner is a lifesaver for folks who have none in their home or anyone who needs to cool off after walking around the town all day. You can see folks from all over the town and at every age in the library. There are classes you can take, there are programs you can attend. Another great thing is that books are not the only thing you can borrow from the library because they have movies, sewing machines, and a whole bunch of other useful items. I have spent so much time in the library growing up in this town. It always offered a safe place for my friends and I to be.
story_A: That is hard to say. The library is one of the only places in my town where an unhoused person can spend lots of their time without being kicked out. They can use the bathrooms and water fountains and find something to entertain themselves. The librarians also know about programs offered by the town so there are great resources available if anyone needs them. I can't think of a safer place in my town for everyone who lives here. I don't even feel safe near the police station.

I would remove the non-owner occupied STR permits. I would make it a space that people want to live. Surrounding the gun shots, I would 100000% enact common sense gun laws. place_A: The Harmonious Legacy Museum, located at 102 East Reconciliation Way in Melodia's Arts District, is a public museum and archive dedicated to the life and legacy of American folk musician and singer-songwriter Woody Guthrie.
why_A: A few years ago, I visited the Harmonious Legacy Museum in Melodia, Oklahoma. As a passionate folk music and American history buff, I was excited to explore the life and legacy of Woody Guthrie. Upon entering the museum, I was greeted by a palpable warmth. It wasn't just the display of artifacts and photographs that moved me, but the attitude of the people who worked there.A volunteer guide, with silver hair and a friendly smile, escorted me on a personalized tour. As we walked through the rooms, she shared stories about Woody, his music and his commitment to social justice. Not only did he talk about historical facts, but he also shared his own experiences and personal connections to Guthrie's music.In a corner of the museum, I found an old guitar with a label that read, "Feel free to play." I hesitated at first, but the guide encouraged me. So I picked up the guitar and began strumming a few notes of "This Land Is Your Land." Other visitors joined in, some singing, others playing improvised instruments. It didn't matter if we were professional or amateur musicians; we were all part of a community united by music and a passion for justice.That afternoon, as I played guitar at the Harmonious Legacy Museum, I felt like I belonged. It didn't matter my background, my musical ability or my political beliefs. I was surrounded by people who shared my love of music and my desire for a more equal world. Woody Guthrie's music brought us together, and in that moment, I felt truly welcomed for who I was.The Harmonious Legacy Museum is not just a museum; it is a space where stories intertwine, where music transcends barriers, and where we are all part of a great song of hope and change. That sense of belonging and acceptance is what makes this place so special to me and to many others who visit.
story_A: Multilingual Signage: Adding signs in various languages ensures that all visitors can understand instructions and relevant information. This includes not only English but also other languages commonly spoken in the local community.Inclusive Rest Areas: Creating rest areas with accessible seating for individuals with reduced mobility is essential. These spaces should be shaded and equipped with comfortable benches, allowing everyone to enjoy the park without restrictions.Cultural and Educational Programs: Organizing events and workshops celebrating cultural diversity and promoting mutual understanding would enhance inclusivity. Music performances, dance, art, and culinary showcases from different cultures could foster a sense of community.Accessible Information: Providing brochures or informational panels that highlight the place’s history and significance from an inclusive perspective would benefit all visitors. Understanding the cultural and social relevance of the Harmonious Legacy Museum contributes to a richer experience for everyone.

place_A: Although the street improvement construction is finished and the street has been open for some time, this is a picture of Evergreen Pizza Station, my favorite pizza restaurant anywhere and an icon and landmark in our neighborhood. It is the renovation of what used to be the actual train station in the community many years ago. The pizza is the best and I have been going there for well over 30 years since it opened.
why_A: This restaurant not only makes the best pizza, it has always been a place that people come for take out as well as eating in, although that changed somewhat during the pandemic. But they opened a patio and have music in the summer, they are extremely community-minded and contribute significantly to the community through programs such as giving free pizza to some community workers, furnishing pizza for events, and for me personally, because I have hosted several get togethers in their dining room, they have always given me a really good price and provided excellent service. I had my daughter's 10th birthday party there, a dinner for out-of-towners at her Bat Mitzvah, and just recently hosted a get-together for our friends to come to meet her new baby because she now lives in another state and was visiting. The owner frequently offers specials at reduced prices, and for years they have had a lunch special really reasonably priced for two pieces of pizza and a drink.
story_A: Honestly, I can't think of anyone who would feel excluded. We are a very integrated neighborhood and this place has always been welcoming to everyone plus so active in our community. During the pandemic when no one could eat in they drastically reduced the size of their dining room and I would return it to its original size if I could because the big screen TVs are gone (which could be annoying (!) but which were also great for folks to come watch football games etc) and the space is very cramped and not as conducive to seating a lot of people at one time.

place_A: internet

One particular instance that stands out is when I first moved into the Everglade and was invited to a community potluck event. As the evening unfolded, I felt embraced for who I am, with people expressing genuine curiosity and appreciation for my perspectives and experiences. This inclusive environment made me feel instantly accepted and valued as a member of the community.

This could involve organizing workshops or training sessions to raise awareness about unconscious biases and promote cultural sensitivity. Additionally, creating affinity groups or support networks specifically tailored to address the needs and concerns of marginalized communities would provide a safe and inclusive space for everyone to feel heard and valued. Moreover, actively seeking input from underrepresented groups in decision-making processes and ensuring their voices are represented and respected would foster a sense of belonging and inclusivity within the community.

place_A: This photo is a picture of the Verdant Grove Mall, one of the largest malls in the U.S.A. While in recent years the mall has slowly died out a bit, it is still a fun, safe place for those of us who live in the area.

Those of us who grew up with this mall from when it was built remember the old days of hanging out for hours and that is part of the reason why it feels safe - it feels like home. The people who come to this mall are usually from the surrounding areas and we all share a sense of understanding... we're mostly from suburban neighborhoods.As for a story about why it makes me feel safe, a couple of days ago when I went, I took the elevator to the fourth floor and some of the people on the elevator smiled as soon as I walked in and they struck up a conversation with me. In recent years the mall has gotten a bit dirtier as less people come to this mall (mall is generating less revenue so not as much money to use for repairs, cleaning, etc.). There has also been a slight influx of teens from Everwood coming to this area and they can be kind of rowdy, usually hanging around in groups. Better security overall would probably make this space feel more safe.

story_A: What could happen is repaving the areas that are buckling for certain. More Glenswood Park convenient to the trail could get signposted. I would improve the waterway as well for more users signing potential hazards as well. Outlets occasionally would help as well as returning the bike maintenance areas though those themselves needed to get maintained I suppose.
place_B: Tot lot/dog park
why_B: It's a place meant for children (or dogs depending on who you ask). As I have neither, I don't feel like it's a usable space for me in the community. It's also usually a key topic of convo in the neighborhood conversations, which I don't feel like I can participate in.
story_B: I would make it have more activities or build more features that can be used by other adults as well.

place_A: The local library
why_A: I am able to make myself at home here. It is a welcoming environment for everyone and has books for every need.
story_A: The only people who might not feel welcome here are those who can't read. It would feel more welcoming if there were more spaces with books on audio. They could provide spaces with headphones so that people could listen instead of read.
place_B: This is a local church in my town.
why_B: For a church that claims to have an open door, they are not inviting. There are constant signs outside making people not part of their faith feel inferior. They post upsetting signs and are anti LGBTQ and against people not of their religion.
story_B: I would have them hire a new PR person to manage their signage. There are plenty of churches around that use signs to show everyone is invited.

place_A: The Shoreline
why_A: What makes me feel welcome is that all people can go to the Shoreline. It is free and welcomes people of all shapes and sizes. No matter who you are you can enjoy a day at the Shoreline.
story_A: A person who might not feel welcome at the Shoreline is someone who does not feel comfortable in a bathing suit. This is because society tends to criticize people who do not look a certain way. A way to modify this space is for Shoreline goers to smile at others and not make comments about other people's bodies.
place_B: Serenity Cove Beach
why_B: I always feel judged by my appearance when I go there. I want to feel welcome no matter what I look like. I love the beach too.
story_B: Have more people like me. Have less judgement. Have people be kinder.

place_A: This is a photo of the pool area of the Enchanted Haven in downtown.
why_A: This pool is very welcoming because it allows me to swim without the worries of insects divebombing at the pool water. I have a bug phobia so this is a very welcome and calming atmosphere for me. It use to be even more welcoming when I was growing up, as you could pay a small fee to swim there for the day, but they got rid of that policy, which saddens me greatly.
story_A: maybe people who didn't like the openess of the area might feel excluded, so I would add more plants and nook type sitting areas for a bit more privacy for some people.
place_B: A local private golf course
why_B: I feel excluded because the annual fees are exorbitant and it is simply not affordable to me. This course does not allow individuals to come in and play a day of golf. You must join and pay the yearly membership.
story_B: Offer a lower annual fee or a daily pass

place_A: I took a photo of a library and the bookshelves
why_A: Whenever I visit the Crystal Haven Library, I feel welcomed for who I am because of the inclusive atmosphere it provides. The vast array of books and resources available here reflects the Crystal Haven Library's commitment to embracing different perspectives and interests. One time, I witnessed a group discussion in a cozy corner of the Crystal Haven Library where people from diverse backgrounds were engaging in a meaningful dialogue about a book they had all read. It was heartwarming to see strangers connecting through literature and ideas, and it reinforced my belief that this space encourages open-mindedness and respect for all.
story_A: In order to promote inclusivity at the Crystal Haven Library, it's important to consider modifications that would make it more accessible for individuals who may feel excluded, such as those with disabilities. This could include installing wheelchair ramps and ensuring there are accessible restrooms to accommodate those with mobility challenges. Providing assistive technologies such as screen readers and large print materials can also improve accessibility for visually impaired patrons. Additionally, offering quiet spaces or sensory-friendly zones can create a more welcoming environment for individuals with sensory sensitivities. All of these modifications can help make the Crystal Haven Library a place where everyone feels welcome and included.
place_B: Store in our little Emerald Bay Mall
why_B: I have never felt welcomed in this place. I get wired looks every time I walk in. I remember one specific time that I could feel all of the people staring and I was trying to figure out what the deal was. When I looked around, I noticed that I was the only guy. Maybe it's not a guy store 🤷🏼‍♂️
story_B: I feel that the staff sets the precedent in this scenario. If the store had some staff that was male, that might help. At least then I wouldn't feel so out of place.

place_A: This is a photo of the rose gardens at Eldoria University, which are nearby
why_A: Its a really peaceful walk surrounded by flowers (in the spring and summer) and other natural desert flora year-round. its always really relaxing and makes you feel connected to nature, even for a brief moment.
story_A: people who dont like plants as much may not like it, but there are paths that are much more stone and desert, if it is more their style.
place_B: This is the community nonprofit where I work in Eldoria. There's a hotel that's just been built to the left.
why_B: I've worked for almost my entire career at a community nonprofit. Then nonprofit is in Eldoria which is great for our clients because it's easy to access. The city where I live has become very very touristy over the last 20 years. Eldoria now feels like a place that's only for tourists and a place that isn't like the community. The hotel that's been built next to our current location makes me feel like this community center doesn't belong and that people like me who lead grassroots organizations that don't cater to tourists don't belong in Eldoria anymore. We're actually having to move the organization. The neighborhood no longer fits our needs and doesn't feel welcoming to us or to our clients.
story_B: This space could look less like a concrete jungle. More and more buildings are being built around long-established nonprofits. As parking decks and tall hotels tower over historic buildings it becomes more and more clear that the people of my town aren't what tourists want to see. Making the space more comfortable for the community with seating, greenery, trees and other things that make a place look lived in would be helpful.

place_A: I took a photo of Silverstone City.
why_A: Nobody really cares about you in this city. Everybody is just too preoccupied with their own lives. I am welcome to be whoever I want to be, without being judge. It is because people do not care that I feel welcomed to be myself.
story_A: I think people who are not aware of the climate of this city. Also, people of different backgrounds and political agendas. They might feel left out and excluded.
place_B: Police academy in my neighborhood
why_B: Some marginalized people have has a bad experience with police. Me personally, aside from those negative experiences, I've also had negative experiences with people at the Evergreen Training Center. As they train, they take over public Meadowland space which ends up feeling like they are pushing away space for locals who frequent the Meadowland like me. Also, one time, someone from the Evergreen Training Center almost ran me over with their car.
story_B: I think the Evergreen Training Center should do more to outreach to the neighborhood

place_A: Eagle Crest Colorado
why_A: This is a great little town just a bit northwest of downtown Denver. It is an old mining town that has been turned into a Casino Gambling resort. It has over 35 casinos in the area. It is very friendly and has no smoking at the majority of the casinos. It is also very easy to walk around. Whenever we are there we feel safe and have a great time.
story_A: People that wouldn't like this are are those that 1. Don't like driving up windy mountain roads that have deep drop offs down to a rushing river. 2. Those that don't like to gamble. 3. That don't like spending a lot of money on food. 4. That don't like having amenities like laundromats, grocery stores and gas stations immediately in the vicinity.

place_A: This is the Willow Valley community lake center within our neighborhood
why_A: The open environment (no surrounding fences), the water, the greenery and the local fauna (geese, ducks) offer an oasis like vibe given we are in the desert.  People of all sorts roam the grounds along the water or sit on benches feeding the ducks like a Norman Rockwell painting.  And with the pool and community event hall, the place can get lively and inviting.
story_A: I wouldn`t modify the space, but would rather hold more frequent events that cater to a variety of interests so that people of all walks can find their niche.  They have Halloween film nights outside, pool parties, senior nights.  I can only imagine that something with a little creativity and earnest could find something for everyone.
place_B: This is the entrance area to a neighborhood dog walking park and off leash run.
why_B: Beware non-dog walkers! If you don't have a pooch with you and want to cross this field to get to other areas of the park, be prepared for suspicious and downright dirty glances from folks frolicking with their fur babies. Dog parents get very territorial and leery of people lurking about without a canine.
story_B: The only way is if the Maplewood council agreed to put up a sign admonishing dog owners to be respectful toward those without dogs.

place_A: It's a park in Glimmerdale.
why_A: This park has a really serene and enjoyable environment. The atmosphere of the park really allows me to think about my problems and future plans without any interactions.
story_A: My little cousins. The wouldn't like this environment because they do not feel comfortable around very quiet areas.
place_B: The Glimmering Grove nearest to my house
why_B: I am 30 with no kids, I feel like I would be seen as weird or suspicious for being here by myself. As for the story, just parking here and getting out to take a picture made me feel like someone might worry that I am a weirdo. Luckily there weren't any kids here, because that would be double weirdo territory.
story_B: It would be cool if our community held more events here. It's a pretty big open space, there could be adult sports, community barbecues, neighborhood garage sales..  maybe I should host one... 🤔

place_A: Fictional Park, in Fantasy City, Imaginaria.
why_A: I grew up in Fantasy City. My family and I often visited this park when I was a child. I associate this place with fun, community, and safety.
story_A: I would add benches where a person could sit. I would also add a trash can so that people could eat snacks and be able to dispose of their trash. Additionally, there should be a shaded area where people can sit out of the sun and be able to enjoy the space much more comfortably.
place_B: I took a picture of the streets I walk on to get to the local Meadow Grove.
why_B: Because I love to walk a lot these streets make me and my family feel excluded because there are no sidewalks. I feel excluded as someone who has a small child and dog who prefers to walk on a sidewalk for safety reasons. Other people without kids may not mind as much as I do as a mom.
story_B: I would love to add sidewalks to both sides of the road on all the streets in my neighborhood.

place_A: The local library
why_A: I am able to make myself at home here. It is a welcoming environment for everyone and has books for every need.
story_A: The only people who might not feel welcome here are those who can't read. It would feel more welcoming if there were more spaces with books on audio. They could provide spaces with headphones so that people could listen instead of read.
place_B: A church
why_B: For me, this place is always super busy on the weekends, but I don't feel like I belong. I'm not particularly religious, and when I walk by, sometimes I feel like people look at me oddly. I think the spaces not as welcome and because of that. Part of me thinks its because of the way I dress. Im fairly athletic and I often wearing running shorts and tank tops as soon as the weather allows (for me. Its comfortable, and I hate sweating) and I think that might be part of the reason. Its not an issue when Im in a workout area but I think walking past big crowds causes some looks.
story_B: Maybe putting a sign outside, or opening the doors and providing more educational information. I would also say making it so that there's more accessible parking in the area and making it easier for people to learn what's happening inside because the location is a little bit on top of the Hill of Dreams.

place_A: The porch of a cabin at Evergreen Peak State Park
why_A: It is a small but accessible park that is not fancy or technologically loaded. There is a nice family restaurant and a lake for fishing and nature trails. There is a little playground for kids and everyone that is there is really friendly and helpful.
story_A: Maybe make some of the signage in Spanish for the Spanish speaking people that may want to visit or stay there. I would also maybe modernize some of the public areas. A lot of what is here has been there for many many decades and could use some TLC to make people feel a little more comfortable using them. People that are used to more fancy or modern things may feel better that way.
place_B: Tot lot/dog park
why_B: It's a place meant for children (or dogs depending on who you ask). As I have neither, I don't feel like it's a usable space for me in the community. It's also usually a key topic of convo in the neighborhood conversations, which I don't feel like I can participate in.
story_B: I would make it have more activities or build more features that can be used by other adults as well.

place_A: My local art center.
why_A: My wife has taken several classes at the art center, which she has used to develop her painting and drawing skills. In the process, she made a friend of one of the instructors, and is now part of an art group that she runs, which enabled her to get her own art show. I too have joined the group, which allowed me to partake in that opportunity (as I had four pieces in it myself). The center is always running innovative events, that draw a cross-section of the community -- for example, they started a 'zine library, and accepted a couple of the works that my wife and I created. This is another example of how the center makes you feel welcome and wanted, no matter what your artistic style, or preference.
story_A: I would say, be mindful of non-regular visitors who may be unfamiliar with the culture and practices of the center. Tailor events with them in mind, like "icebreaker" type of activities that don't cost a lot of money, or end up being scheduled during working hours, when most people with nine to five schedules can't make it. In my experience, the less open the schedule feels, or the heftier the price structure comes across, the more alienating it seems, even if that's not the intention.
place_B: The Mystic Academy
why_B: There really aren't places in my community where I wouldn't be welcome. But I'm old enough now that middle schoolers or high schoolers would see me as odd. My kids are young adults so I don't even know what the teens like. It would purely be an age difference that would make me be excluded.
story_B: It would probably require me to be a part of that space to have teens be more welcoming. Getting to know each other would help that.

place_A: A pond in my city
why_A: It is usually very quiet and peaceful. The ducks are always happy to see me since I feed them often. I love it.
story_A: I would keep it simple. Perhaps incorporate a few exercise equipments
place_B: This is the entrance area to a neighborhood dog walking park and off leash run.
why_B: Beware non-dog walkers! If you don't have a pooch with you and want to cross this field to get to other areas of the park, be prepared for suspicious and downright dirty glances from folks frolicking with their fur babies. story_B: The only way is if the Maplewood council agreed to put up a sign admonishing dog owners to be respectful toward those without dogs.

place_A: A hiking trail
why_A: Nature always makes me feel supported and accepted. People you come across in the outdoors are typically friendly and helpful.
story_A: People who aren't as physically fit may not feel included. Signs with hikes of varying difficulties (including easy ones) would maybe help.
place_B: Mossvale hiking trail
why_B: I always feel excluded when I go hiking with my friends, because they are all in better shape than I am. It makes me feel excluded because I am always the slow one on these hikes. I also feel excluded because other people often take the dogs hiking. The last time we all went together, my friends had to wait for me to catch up because I was so out of breath, which made me feel excluded.
story_B: I would modify the space to make it less excluding by modifying the terrain. By modifying the train, it would make it easier for me to do the hikes, and less steep. I would feel more welcomed if my friends didn't make me feel bad about needing to slow down.

place_A: At a hotel downtown with a good friend
why_A: This was a high class hotel and restaurant in the downtown area. It was very expensive. But worth the great night.
story_A: It may be hard for people to pay for this night. I could invite them to my table and make conversation. Then offer to pay for their evening.
place_B: Five points bar area
why_B: I'm not a big drinker and I feel like I've aged out of being young of just going to the bars. So I feel like this doesn't really welcome me as a friendly person. I feel like it's very secluded if you're single, and it's really more for people who date.
story_B: I think if they had more events in the area or a diverse menu of non-alcoholic items, it could welcome me. I feel like I don't know anybody who goes here, so maybe I just need mutual friends to introduce me to this fun side of Greyvale

place_A: This is a photo of a Eldergrove Library.
why_A: I have always felt welcomed by Eldergrove Libraries. When I was a teenager, it was helpful to have a quiet place to engage in books and media that I was interested in. I feel that Eldergrove Libraries are one of the last places in modern life where anyone can go and spend time, regardless of whether or not they buy anything.
story_A: I have heard that people experiencing homelessness have sometimes feel excluded in Eldergrove Libraries. I would modify this space by providing them with small services to help them feel welcome. Some Eldergrove Libraries have things like public showers and small amenities like soap that can help people without homes feel more welcome. They also might be less likely to get kicked out due to hygiene.

Maybe it's not a guy store 🤷🏼‍♂️
story_B: I feel that the staff sets the precedent in this scenario. If the store had some staff that was male, that might help. At least then I wouldn't feel so out of place.

place_A: This the Adventure Cove amusement park ride
why_A: It is a fun place where you can feel relaxed there are a lot of attractions from rollercoasters to mini trains and a water park
story_A: Handicapped people to make them feel more included maybe make a ride where you can lock in a wheel chair

place_A: This is a photo of a beach on Pleasant Isle.
why_A: This is the closest ocean beach to where I live, it is a fifteen minute drive and when I get there, it makes me feel like I am on vacation. There are great waves, beautiful sand and lots to do here and I usually go with family and friends. The beach is open year round and I enjoy going in the winter when nobody is there too.
story_A: This is a town beach and only town residents are allowed there but if you come with me in my car, we can spend the day and enjoy ourselves even if you don't live here. When we go, we bring lots of chairs, food and things to do, everyone has a good time. If you don't like to swim, just hang out under the umbrella and read a book or listen to some music.
place_B: A members only club that takes up a chunk of the waterfront in Silverdale.
why_B: It's a very upscale exclusive club. I don't feel like I belong here because I can't afford membership. The parking lot is full of fancy cars and it feels focused on the wealthy people in the community.
story_B: Not have the club be in the middle of the town. I would also some of the members only signs so it isn't as obvious. I would reduce the advertising around town to make it feel more inclusive.

place_A: Fictional City
why_A: It was taken from the main streets of Fictional City, A very serene and splendid adventure and a lovely place to be.
story_A: My brother believes it's too crowded and he hates it and won't ever come close to the area.
place_B: Police academy in my neighborhood

place_A: This was taken in Silver Grove, NJ which has a 9/11 memorial site. This picture shows the view of New Avalon on 9/11 last year.
why_A: We have a 9/11 memorial site in town. This is a view of New Avalon from the memorial site on 9/11 last year.
story_A: Someone that comes from another country may not feel this picture would exclude them.
place_B: The courthouse in Wyndale county where I live
why_B: I used to get in trouble a lot when I was younger and even though I changed a lot and moved away for 10 years when I walked in there the treatment I get is like I'm still that unruly kid from 25 years ago. many many things and stories I could tell that make me feel this way! For instance, a year or 2 ago, I was struggling financially and I did not get my registration (tags) on time. When I got to the courthouse to get them, I was addressed as if I taking all their precious time (that theyre getting paid to do) I could even audibly hear the over exaggerated exhales. And I began explaining what I needed , simply saying I wasnt able to get my tags yet, so I need to get that taken care of please as I was putting my proof of insurance and documents on the counter and getting my checkbook. Well I had given them too much trouble I guess because no sooner that I sat that on the counter 2 ladies both rushed over to explain to me in great detail how I could not use a personal check. (I grew up in this county so any other time, Ill cut and run! I have no interest in participating in the back and forth) BUT the lady directly in front of me (someone I knew and even talked to while in line) used a personal check, absolutely no issues, no one panicked or ran to grossly express how important it is to never bring such a thing to the court house. They just asked for her phone number and put it in the drawer and gave her a receipt. So I decided what the hell and brought it to their attention but again no less than 3 office clerks brazenly educated me on the rules of taking checks for payments. And apparently since I had never used a check before, I wasnt able to pay with my check  HOWEVER, once I had successfully made any payments and the check cleared, then I would be able to use checks going forward. But I cant use a check for the first time if I cant use a check so.. the rules are daunting and it is clear someone of my lowliness could never comprehend.
story_B: It would be great if people weren't as judge mental and understand people can change. It's a small town and it just seems a lot of the people born and raised here decide who is worthy of just general human decency and respect.

place_A: YMCA in Greenvale
why_A: This is a community location that holds many different activities for all ages.  I always feel welcome here because the staff are wonderful.
story_A: I could see how people of color could feel unwelcome.  This is a mostly Greenvale community.  I think hiring some people of color would help to make them feel more welcome.
place_B: A local gym that I tried before the Eldergrove Gym. This is their new location. They just switched one building over. But they weren't very welcoming.
why_B: They weren't very welcoming. Their classes were elite and closed off. I also couldn't get any of the help I sought.
story_B: I would have people available to assist others and make the environment more people friendly rather than the typical gym personality. I didn't even know what the grand opening was for and I stopped in to see. This time around I was nearly 40 lbs lighter and they were extremely friendly and welcoming to me. My size shouldn't have made a difference.

place_A: I’m in my own home. I don’t feel super welcome elsewhere.
why_A: Because it’s just me and my wife and my pets. I’m non binary and autistic, so I feel very self conscious almost everywhere. Home is where I can relax and be myself.
story_A: My mother. She hasn’t spoken to me in years. I imagine she may be very uncomfortable on my Glimmering Cove.
place_B: I am at the Melody Hall. I love classical music but don't feel like I fit the mold of the people in my community who come here.
why_B: I love classical music and go to the Melody Hall when I can. While they say they want to build a younger and more diverse audience, they tend to be all older people who scoff at people who are dressed down/relaxed, come with kids, etc.
story_B: While I think the place itself is inclusive, I don't think the old people who are the regulars have gotten the message. I think it's more about educating everyone that the only path to survival is to be inclusive.
